steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The claim is that New York society prefers familiarity over novelty. The correct quotation must show the society actively choosing something familiar when a newer alternative is available — a direct contrast between old and new, with the old winning.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘"But, in the first place, New York was a metropolis, and perfectly aware that in metropolises it was 'not the thing' to arrive early at the opera."’: Describes a social convention about arrival timing. No contrast between old and new, no preference illustrated. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘"It was Madame Nilsson's first appearance [in an opera] that winter, and what the daily press had already learned to describe as 'an exceptionally brilliant audience' had gathered to hear her."’: Emphasizes the prestige of the audience and the novelty of the performer’s first appearance. This highlights novelty, not a preference for familiarity. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘"To come to the Opera in a [carriage for hire] was almost as honourable a way of arriving as in one's own carriage."’: Discusses how people arrived, not which venue they chose or a preference between old and new. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘"Though there was already talk...of a new Opera House which should compete in costliness and splendour with those of the great European capitals, the world of fashion was still content to reassemble every winter in the shabby red and gold boxes of the sociable old Academy [of Music in New York]."’: Directly contrasts a new, grander option with the old venue — and shows society choosing the familiar old Academy anyway. Perfectly illustrates preferring familiarity over novelty. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
